documents.title: Antibiotic Alternatives and Usage
documents.content: Reserve true antibiotics for potentially lethal infections (sepsis, severe pneumonia). Never under-dose—finish the full course to prevent creating resistant superbugs. If antibiotics run out: honey (especially Manuka) on wounds creates an osmotic barrier, iodine inhibits bacterial growth topically, and heavily diluted bleach (Dakin's solution) can sanitize severe surface wounds.

documents.title: Field Dental Emergencies
documents.content: A tooth abscess can become a lethal brain/blood infection. Treat severe pain with clove oil (eugenol) applied directly to the cavity. If extraction is unavoidable: numb if possible, detach the gum with a sharp sterile tool, push tooth back and forth to break periodontal ligaments—do not pull straight out or roots will snap. Pack socket with sterile gauze.

documents.title: Unassisted Childbirth basics
documents.content: Prepare a clean environment. Do NOT pull on the baby during delivery. Support the perineum to prevent tearing. After birth, clear the baby's airway (mouth then nose). Keep the baby warm and place skin-to-skin immediately. Do not pull the umbilical cord; wait for the placenta to birth naturally (usually 10-30 mins). Tie off the cord tightly in two places and cut between them with a sterilized blade.

documents.title: Waterborne Disease Management
documents.content: Cholera, dysentery, and Giardia kill via severe dehydration. Do not use anti-diarrheal drugs immediately; the body is flushing pathogens. Instead, constantly administer Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS): 1 liter safe water, 6 level teaspoons sugar, 0.5 level teaspoon salt. Sip slowly to prevent vomiting.

documents.title: Wound Closure Decisions
documents.content: Do NOT suture a dirty wound, an animal/human bite, or a puncture wound. Closing bacteria inside leads to lethal infections. Leave dirty wounds open, pack with sterile gauze wet with saline, and change daily (let it heal from the bottom up). Only suture or superglue clean, straight lacerations within 6-8 hours of injury.
